Q1 | Mechanics Easydiagram
ok conf:1.00
From a disc of radius R and mass M, a circular hole of diameter R, whose rim passes through the centre is cut. What is the moment of inertia of the remaining part of the disc about a perpendicular axis, passing through the centre?
(1) 15 MR^2/32
(2) 13 MR^2/32 ✓ Correct
(3) 11 MR^2/32
(4) 9 MR^2/32
Solution: Moment of inertia of the complete disc about an axis passing through its center and perpendicular to its plane is I_disc = MR^2/2. For the cut portion, the radius is R/2 and mass is m = M_disc * (Area_hole / Area_disc) = M * (π(R/2)^2 / πR^2) = M/4. Moment of inertia of the cut portion about its own center (which is also the center of the original disc) is I_hole_center = m(R/2)^2 / 2 = (M/4)(R/2)^2 / 2 = M R^2 / 32. Using the parallel axis theorem, the moment of inertia of the cut portion about the center of the original disc (since its rim passes through the center) I_hole_parallel = I_hole_...
[solution diagram]

Q2 | Electromagnetism Mediumdiagram
ok conf:1.00
A square loop ABCD carrying a current i, is placed near and coplanar with a long straight conductor XY carrying a current I, the net force on the loop will be:
[question diagram]

(1) 2μ₀Ii/3π ✓ Correct
(2) μ₀Ii/2π
(3) 2μ₀IiL/3π
(4) μ₀IiL/2π
Solution: The force on a current-carrying wire of length L due to a long straight current-carrying wire is F = (μ₀ I_1 I_2 L) / (2πd). The force between parallel currents is attractive, and between anti-parallel currents is repulsive. For side AD, distance d_1 = L/2, currents I and i are in opposite directions (repulsive). F_AD = (μ₀IiL) / (2π(L/2)) = (μ₀Ii)/π. For side BC, distance d_2 = L/2 + L = 3L/2, currents I and i are in the same direction (attractive). F_BC = (μ₀IiL) / (2π(3L/2)) = (μ₀Ii)/(3π). The forces on sides AB and CD are equal and opposite, so they cancel out. Net force F_net = F_AD - F_B...
[solution diagram]

Q3 | Electromagnetism Medium
ok conf:1.00
The magnetic susceptibility is negative for:
(1) diamagnetic material only ✓ Correct
(2) paramagnetic material only
(3) ferromagnetic material only
(4) paramagnetic and ferromagnetic materials
Solution: Magnetic susceptibility χ_m is negative for diamagnetic substance only.
Q4 | Waves Mediumdiagram
ok conf:1.00
A siren emitting a sound of frequency 800 Hz moves away from an observer towards a cliff at a speed of 15 ms¯¹. Then, the frequency of sound that the observer hears in the echo reflected from the cliff is: (Take velocity of sound in air = 330 ms¯¹)
(1) 765 Hz
(2) 800 Hz
(3) 838 Hz ✓ Correct
(4) 885 Hz
Solution: Given f_0 = 800 Hz, V_source = 15 m/s (moving away from observer), V_sound = 330 m/s. The observer hears the echo reflected from the cliff. First, consider the cliff as the observer. The frequency heard by the cliff is f_cliff = f_0 * (V_sound / (V_sound - (-V_source))) = f_0 * (V_sound / (V_sound + V_source)) (since source is moving away from cliff, effective distance increases). So, f_cliff = 800 * (330 / (330 + 15)) = 800 * (330 / 345). Now, the cliff acts as a stationary source emitting sound at f_cliff, and the observer is moving towards it (because the original source was moving away, th...
[solution diagram]

Q5 | Current electricity Mediumdiagram
ok conf:1.00
A capacitor of 2µF is charged as shown in the diagram. When the switch S is turned to position 2, the percentage of its stored energy dissipated is:
[question diagram]

(1) 0%
(2) 20%
(3) 75%
(4) 80% ✓ Correct
Solution: Initial charge on 2µF capacitor = Q = C * V = 2µF * V. Initial stored energy U_initial = 1/2 * C * V^2 = 1/2 * (2µF) * V^2 = V^2 µJ. When switch S is turned to position 2, the 2µF capacitor is connected in parallel with 8µF capacitor. Total capacitance C_total = 2µF + 8µF = 10µF. The total charge remains Q = 2V µC. Final voltage across parallel combination V_final = Q/C_total = (2V µC) / (10µF) = V/5 V. Final stored energy U_final = 1/2 * C_total * V_final^2 = 1/2 * (10µF) * (V/5)^2 = 5µF * V^2/25 = V^2/5 µJ. Energy dissipated = U_initial - U_final = V^2 - V^2/5 = 4V^2/5 µJ. Percentage dissipa...
[solution diagram]


Q6 | Optics Hard
ok conf:1.00
In a diffraction pattern due to a single slit of width ‘a', the first minimum is observed at an angle 30°when light of wavelength 5000 Å is incident on the slit. The first secondary maximum is observed at an angle of:
(1) sin⁻¹(1/4)
(2) sin⁻¹(2/3)
(3) sin⁻¹(1/2)
(4) sin⁻¹(3/4) ✓ Correct
Solution: For the first minimum in single slit diffraction, the condition is a sin θ = λ. Given θ = 30°, so a sin 30° = λ => a(1/2) = λ => a = 2λ. For the first secondary maximum, the condition is a sin θ' = (3/2)λ. Substitute a = 2λ: (2λ) sin θ' = (3/2)λ => sin θ' = (3/2) * (1/2) = 3/4. Therefore, the angle is θ' = sin⁻¹(3/4).
Q7 | Gravitation Hard
ok conf:1.00
At what height from the surface of earth the gravitation potential and the value of g are -5.4 × 10⁷J kg⁻² and 6.0 ms⁻² respectively? Take the radius of earth as 6400 km:
(1) 2600 km ✓ Correct
(2) 1600 km
(3) 1400 km
(4) 2000 km
Solution: Gravitational potential V = -GM/(R+h) = -5.4 × 10⁷ J/kg. Acceleration due to gravity g' = GM/(R+h)² = 6.0 m/s². Dividing the potential by g': V/g' = (-GM/(R+h)) / (GM/(R+h)²) = -(R+h). So, R+h = -V/g' = -(-5.4 × 10⁷) / 6.0 = (5.4 × 10⁷) / 6 = 0.9 × 10⁷ m = 9 × 10⁶ m = 9000 km. Given radius of Earth R = 6400 km. Therefore, height h = (R+h) - R = 9000 km - 6400 km = 2600 km.
Q8 | Electromagnetism Medium
ok conf:1.00
Out of the following options which one can be used to produce a propagating electromagnetic wave?
(1) A charge moving at constant velocity
(2) A stationary charge
(3) A chargeless particle
(4) An accelerating charge ✓ Correct
Solution: A propagating electromagnetic wave is produced by an accelerating charge. A stationary charge produces an electric field. A charge moving at constant velocity produces both electric and magnetic fields, but no electromagnetic waves. A chargeless particle does not produce any electromagnetic fields.
Q9 | Electrostatics Harddiagram
ok conf:1.00
Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths l, are initially at a distance d (d < < l) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity u. Then u varies as a function of the distance x between the spheres, as
(1) u ∝ x²
(2) u ∝ x
(3) u ∝ x⁻¹⁄² ✓ Correct
(4) u ∝ x⁻¹
Solution: From equilibrium of forces on a sphere: F_e = mg tan θ. For small angles, tan θ ≈ sin θ ≈ x/(2l). So, F_e = mgx/(2l). Coulomb force F_e = kq²/x². Thus, kq²/x² = mgx/(2l) => q² = (mgx³)/(2lk). Differentiating with respect to time t: 2q (dq/dt) = (mg/(2lk)) * 3x² (dx/dt). Given dq/dt is constant (let's call it C). dx/dt = u. So, 2qC = (3mg/(2lk)) x²u. From q² = (mgx³)/(2lk), q = sqrt(mg/(2lk)) x^(3/2). Substitute q into the differentiated equation: 2 * sqrt(mg/(2lk)) x^(3/2) * C = (3mg/(2lk)) x²u. This simplifies to u ∝ x^(3/2) / x² = x^(3/2 - 2) = x^(-1/2). So, u ∝ x^(-1/2).
[solution diagram]

Q10 | Waves Mediumdiagram
ok conf:1.00
A uniform rope of length L and mass m₁, hangs vertically from a rigid support. A block of mass m₂ is attached to the free end of the rope. A transverse pulse of wavelength λ₁ is produced at the lower end of the rope. The wavelength of the pulse when it reaches the top of the rope is λ₂. The ratio λ₂/λ₁ is:
(1) m₁/m₂
(2) √(m₁+m₂)/m₂ ✓ Correct
(3) m₂/(m₁+m₂)
(4) m₁/(m₁+m₂)
Solution: The speed of a transverse wave in a string is v = √(T/μ), where T is the tension and μ is the linear mass density (μ = m₁/L). The wavelength λ = v/f. Since the frequency f of the pulse remains constant, λ ∝ v ∝ √T. At the lower end of the rope (where the block m₂ is attached), the tension T₁ = m₂g. So, λ₁ ∝ √(m₂g). At the top of the rope, the tension T₂ = (m₁ + m₂)g (due to the mass of the rope and the block). So, λ₂ ∝ √((m₁ + m₂)g). The ratio λ₂/λ₁ = √(T₂/T₁) = √((m₁ + m₂)g / (m₂g)) = √((m₁ + m₂)/m₂).
[solution diagram]

Q11 | Thermal physics Easydiagram
ok conf:1.00
A refrigerator works between 4°C and 30°C. It is required to remove 600 calories of heat every second in order to keep the temperature of the refrigerated space constant. The power required is: (Take 1 cal = 4.2 Joules)
(1) 2.365 W
(2) 23.65 W
(3) 236.5 W ✓ Correct
(4) 2365 W
Solution: Given cold temperature T_C = 4°C = 277 K. Hot temperature T_H = 30°C = 303 K. Heat removed from cold reservoir Q_C = 600 calories/s = 600 * 4.2 J/s = 2520 J/s. The coefficient of performance (COP) of a refrigerator is given by COP = Q_C / W = T_C / (T_H - T_C). So, W = Q_C * (T_H - T_C) / T_C. W = 2520 J/s * (303 K - 277 K) / 277 K = 2520 * (26 / 277) J/s = 236.5 J/s = 236.5 W.
[solution diagram]

Q12 | Waves Medium
ok conf:1.00
An air column, closed at one end and open at the other, resonates with a tuning fork when the smallest length of the column is 50 cm. The next larger length of the column resonating with the same tuning fork is:
(1) 66.7 cm
(2) 100 cm
(3) 150cm ✓ Correct
(4) 200 cm
Solution: For a closed organ pipe (one end closed, one end open), the resonant lengths are given by L = (2n-1)λ/4, where n=1, 2, 3,... For the smallest length (n=1), L_1 = λ/4. Given L_1 = 50 cm, so λ = 4 * 50 cm = 200 cm. The next larger length for resonance (n=2) is L_2 = (2*2-1)λ/4 = 3λ/4. Substitute λ = 200 cm: L_2 = 3 * (200 cm) / 4 = 3 * 50 cm = 150 cm.
Q13 | Modern physics Harddiagram
ok conf:1.00
Consider the junction diode as ideal. The value of current flowing through AB is:
[question diagram]

(1) 0 A
(2) 10⁻² A ✓ Correct
(3) 10⁻¹ A
(4) 10⁻³ A
Solution: Given V_A = +4 V and V_B = -6 V. Since V_A > V_B, the p-n junction diode is forward biased. As the diode is ideal, it acts as a short circuit (zero resistance). The potential difference across the 1 kΩ resistor is ΔV = V_A - V_B = 4 V - (-6 V) = 10 V. The resistance is R = 1 kΩ = 1000 Ω. Using Ohm's law, the current I = ΔV / R = 10 V / 1000 Ω = 0.01 A = 10⁻² A.
Q14 | Electrostatics Medium
ok conf:1.00
The charge flowing through a resistance R varies with time t as Q = at - bt² where a and b are positive constants. The total heat produced in R is:
(1) a³R/6b ✓ Correct
(2) a³R/3b
(3) a³R/2b
(4) a³R/b
Solution: Given charge Q = at - bt². Current i = dQ/dt = a - 2bt. Heat produced H = ∫ i²R dt. The current flows until it becomes zero and then reverses direction. The total heat produced is due to the magnitude of current, so we integrate from t=0 to the time when i=0. Setting i=0: a - 2bt = 0 => t = a/(2b). H = ∫[0 to a/(2b)] (a - 2bt)² R dt. Let u = a - 2bt. Then du = -2b dt. When t=0, u=a. When t=a/(2b), u=0. H = ∫[a to 0] u² R (-du/(2b)) = (R/(2b)) ∫[0 to a] u² du = (R/(2b)) * [u³/3]_0^a = (R/(2b)) * (a³/3 - 0) = (a³R)/(6b).
Q15 | Thermal physics Hard
ok conf:1.00
A black body is at a temperature of 5760 K. The energy of radiation emitted by the body at wavelength 250 nm is U₁, at wavelength 500 nm is U₂ and that at 1000 nm is U₃. Wien's constant, b = 2.88 × 10⁶ nmk. Which of the following is correct?
(1) U₁ = 0
(2) U₃ = 0
(3) U₁ > U₂ ✓ Correct
(4) U₂ > U₁
Solution: Wien's displacement law states that λ_max T = b. Given T = 5760 K and b = 2.88 × 10⁶ nm K. So, λ_max = b/T = (2.88 × 10⁶ nm K) / (5760 K) = 500 nm. This means the spectral emissive power is maximum at 500 nm. Therefore, U₂ (at 500 nm) is the peak energy. This implies that U₁ (at 250 nm) < U₂ and U₃ (at 1000 nm) < U₂. However, if one uses the approximation u_λ ∝ (1/λ)⁴ as implicitly suggested in some contexts (though not strictly Planck's law for all wavelengths), then U₁ (at 250 nm) would be greater than U₂ (at 500 nm) since 250 nm is smaller than 500 nm. Following this approximation from the ...
Q16 | Solid and fluid mecahnics Medium
ok conf:1.00
Coefficient of linear expansion of brass and steel rods are α₁ and α₂. Lengths of brass and steel rods are l₁ and l₂ respectively. If (l₂ - l₁) is maintained same at all temperatures, which one of the following relations holds good?
(1) α₁²l₁ = α₂²l₂
(2) α₁l₁ = α₂l₂²
(3) α₁l₂ = α₂l₁
(4) α₁l₁ = α₂l₂ ✓ Correct
Solution: The change in length of a rod due to temperature change ΔT is Δl = l₀αΔT, where l₀ is the initial length and α is the coefficient of linear expansion. Given that (l₂ - l₁) is maintained constant at all temperatures. This means that the change in length of the two rods must be equal for any given temperature change. So, Δl₁ = Δl₂. l₁α₁ΔT = l₂α₂ΔT. Since ΔT is common and non-zero, we have l₁α₁ = l₂α₂.
Q17 | Modern physics Medium
ok conf:1.00
A npn transistor is connected in common emitter configuration in a given amplifier. A load resistance of 800 Ω is connected in the collector circuit and the voltage drop across it is 0.8 V. If the current amplification factor is 0.96 and the input resistance of the circuit is 192 Ω, the voltage gain and the power gain of the amplifier will respectively be:
(1) 4,3.84 ✓ Correct
(2) 3.69, 3.84
(3) 4, 4
(4) 4, 3.69
Solution: Given load resistance R_C = 800 Ω, voltage drop across R_C = V_C = 0.8 V. Collector current I_C = V_C / R_C = 0.8 V / 800 Ω = 0.001 A. Current amplification factor β = I_C / I_B = 0.96. Input resistance R_in = 192 Ω. Voltage gain A_V = (V_out / V_in) = (I_C R_C) / (I_B R_in). Since I_B = I_C / β, A_V = (I_C R_C) / ((I_C / β) R_in) = β * (R_C / R_in). A_V = 0.96 * (800 Ω / 192 Ω) = 0.96 * (25/6) = 0.96 * 4.166... ≈ 4. Voltage gain = 4. Power gain A_P = A_V * β = 4 * 0.96 = 3.84. Thus, the voltage gain and power gain are 4 and 3.84 respectively.
Q18 | Optics Medium
ok conf:1.00
The intensity at the maximum in a Young's double slit experiment is I₀. Distance between two slits is d = 5λ, where λ is the wavelength of light used in the experiment. What will be the intensity in front of one of the slits on the screen placed at a distance D = 10 d?
(1) I₀
(2) I₀/4
(3) I₀
(4) I₀/2 ✓ Correct
Solution: In Young's Double Slit Experiment (YDSE), the maximum intensity is I_max = I₀. The intensity at any point is given by I = I_max cos²(Φ/2), where Φ is the phase difference. The phase difference Φ = (2π/λ)Δx, where Δx is the path difference. The path difference Δx = yd/D. We need to find the intensity in front of one of the slits. Let the center of the screen be y=0. If one slit is at -d/2 and the other at +d/2, then the point in front of one of the slits corresponds to y = d/2. So, Δx = (d/2) * (d/D) = d²/(2D). Given d = 5λ and D = 10d. Substitute D: Δx = d²/(2 * 10d) = d/(20). Substitute d = 5...
Q19 | Mechanics Easy
ok conf:1.00
A uniform circular disc of radius 50 cm at rest is free to turn about an axis which is perpendicular to its plane and passes through its centre. It is subjected to a torque which produces a constant angular acceleration of 2.0 rad s⁻². Its net acceleration in ms⁻² at the end of 2.0 s is approximately:
(1) 8.0 ✓ Correct
(2) 7.0
(3) 6.0
(4) 3.0
Solution: Given radius R = 50 cm = 0.5 m, initial angular velocity ω₀ = 0 rad/s, angular acceleration α = 2.0 rad/s². At t = 2.0 s, the angular velocity ω = ω₀ + αt = 0 + (2.0 rad/s²)(2.0 s) = 4.0 rad/s. At any point on the rim of the disc, there are two components of acceleration: tangential acceleration (a_t) and centripetal acceleration (a_c). Tangential acceleration a_t = Rα = (0.5 m)(2.0 rad/s²) = 1.0 m/s². Centripetal acceleration a_c = Rω² = (0.5 m)(4.0 rad/s)² = 0.5 * 16 m/s² = 8.0 m/s². The net acceleration a_net = √(a_t² + a_c²) = √((1.0)² + (8.0)²) = √(1 + 64) = √65 ≈ 8.06 m/s². The closest o...
Q20 | Dual nature of radiation and matter Easy
ok conf:1.00
An electron of mass m and a photon have same energy E. the ratio of de-Broglie wavelengths associated with them is: (c being velocity of light)
(1) (1/c)√(E/(2m)) ✓ Correct
(2) √(E/(2mc²))
(3) c√(2mE)
(4) (1/c)√(2m/E)
Solution: For an electron, its kinetic energy is E = p_e²/2m, so momentum p_e = √(2mE). The de-Broglie wavelength for the electron is λ_e = h/p_e = h/√(2mE). For a photon, its energy E = p_p c, so momentum p_p = E/c. The de-Broglie wavelength for the photon is λ_p = h/p_p = hc/E. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelengths (electron to photon) is λ_e/λ_p = (h/√(2mE)) / (hc/E) = (h/√(2mE)) * (E/hc) = E / (c√(2mE)) = √(E) / (c√(2m)) = (1/c)√(E/(2m)).
Q21 | Mechanics Medium
ok conf:1.00
A disk and a sphere of same radius but different masses roll off on two inclined planes of the same altitude and length. Which one of the two objects gets to the bottom of the plane first?
(1) Disk
(2) Sphere ✓ Correct
(3) Both reach at the same time
(4) Depends on their masses
Solution: For an object rolling down an inclined plane, the acceleration is given by a = (g sinθ) / (1 + I/(MR²)). For a solid sphere, the moment of inertia I_sphere = (2/5)MR². So, I_sphere/(MR²) = 2/5. Acceleration of sphere a_sphere = (g sinθ) / (1 + 2/5) = (g sinθ) / (7/5) = (5/7)g sinθ. For a solid disc, the moment of inertia I_disc = (1/2)MR². So, I_disc/(MR²) = 1/2. Acceleration of disc a_disc = (g sinθ) / (1 + 1/2) = (g sinθ) / (3/2) = (2/3)g sinθ. Since 5/7 ≈ 0.714 and 2/3 ≈ 0.667, a_sphere > a_disc. The object with greater acceleration reaches the bottom first. Hence, the sphere will reach the...
Q22 | Optics Easy
ok conf:1.00
The angle of incidence for a ray of light at a refracting surface of a prism is 45°. The angle of prism is 60°. If the ray suffers minimum deviation through the prism, the angle of minimum deviation and refractive index of the material of the prism respectively, are:
(1) 45°; 1/√2
(2) 30°; √2 ✓ Correct
(3) 45°; √2
(4) 30°; 1/√2
Solution: For minimum deviation in a prism, the angle of incidence i and angle of emergence e are equal, and the angle of refraction inside the prism r₁ and r₂ are equal (r₁ = r₂ = r). Also, i = (A + δ_min)/2 and r = A/2. Given i = 45° and angle of prism A = 60°. So, 45° = (60° + δ_min)/2 => 90° = 60° + δ_min => δ_min = 30°. Also, r = A/2 = 60°/2 = 30°. The refractive index μ = sin(i) / sin(r) = sin(45°) / sin(30°) = (1/√2) / (1/2) = 2/√2 = √2. Thus, the angle of minimum deviation is 30° and the refractive index is √2.
Q23 | Modern physics Medium
ok conf:1.00
When an α-particle of mass ‘m’moving with velocity 'v' bombards on a heavy nucleus of charge 'Ze', its distance of closest approach from the nucleus depends on m as:
(1) 1/m ✓ Correct
(2) 1/√m
(3) 1/m²
(4) m
Solution: At the distance of closest approach (r_0), the initial kinetic energy of the α-particle is completely converted into electrostatic potential energy. Initial kinetic energy = (1/2)mv². Potential energy at closest approach = k(q₁q₂)/r_0, where k = 1/(4πε₀). The charge of α-particle q₁ = 2e, and charge of nucleus q₂ = Ze. So, (1/2)mv² = k(2e)(Ze)/r_0 = (2kZe²)/r_0. Rearranging for r_0: r_0 = (4kZe²)/(mv²). From this, it is clear that the distance of closest approach r_0 is inversely proportional to the mass m of the α-particle (r_0 ∝ 1/m).
Q24 | Mechanics Medium
ok conf:1.00
A particle of mass 10 g moves along a circle of radius 6.4 cm with a constant tangential acceleration. What is the magnitude of this acceleration if the kinetic energy of the particle becomes equal to 8 × 10⁻⁴ J by the end of the second revolution after the beginning of the motion?
(1) 0.1 m/s² ✓ Correct
(2) 0.15 m/s²
(3) 0.18 m/s²
(4) 0.2m/s²
Solution: Given mass m = 10 g = 0.01 kg, radius R = 6.4 cm = 0.064 m. Initial angular velocity ω₀ = 0. Angular displacement θ = 2 revolutions = 2 * 2π = 4π rad. Let tangential acceleration be a_t, then angular acceleration α = a_t/R. Using the kinematic equation for rotational motion: ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ. Since ω₀ = 0, ω² = 2(a_t/R)(4π) = 8πa_t/R. The kinetic energy K.E. = (1/2)mv² = (1/2)m(Rω)². Substitute ω²: K.E. = (1/2)mR²(8πa_t/R) = 4πmRa_t. Given K.E. = 8 × 10⁻⁴ J. So, 8 × 10⁻⁴ = 4π * (0.01 kg) * (0.064 m) * a_t. a_t = (8 × 10⁻⁴) / (4π * 0.01 * 0.064) = (8 × 10⁻⁴) / (0.00256π) = 0.1 m/s².
Q25 | Thermal physics Hard
ok conf:1.00
The molecules of a given mass of a gas have r.m.s velocity of 200 ms⁻¹ at 27°C and 1.0 × 10⁵ Nm⁻² pressure. When the temperature and pressure of the gas are respectively, 127°C and 0.05 × 10⁵ Nm⁻², the r.m.s. velocity of its molecules in ms⁻¹ is:
(1) 100√2
(2) 400/√3 ✓ Correct
(3) 100√2/3
(4) 100/3
Solution: The root mean square (RMS) velocity of gas molecules is given by V_rms = √(3RT/M), where R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, and M is the molar mass of the gas. For a given gas, M is constant, so V_rms ∝ √T. Initial conditions: V₁ = 200 m/s at T₁ = 27°C = (27 + 273) K = 300 K. Final conditions: T₂ = 127°C = (127 + 273) K = 400 K. The pressure change does not directly affect V_rms for a given mass and temperature. Ratio of RMS velocities: V₂/V₁ = √(T₂/T₁). V₂ = V₁ * √(T₂/T₁) = 200 m/s * √(400 K / 300 K) = 200 * √(4/3) = 200 * (2/√3) = 400/√3 m/s.
Q26 | Electromagnetism Medium
ok conf:1.00
A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current I. the current is uniformly distributed over its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic fields B and B', at radial distances a/2 and 2a respectively, from the axis of the wire is:
(1) 1/4
(2) 1/2
(3) 1 ✓ Correct
(4) 4
Solution: For a long straight wire of radius 'a' carrying a uniformly distributed current I: The magnetic field inside the wire (r < a) is given by B_in = (μ₀ I r) / (2π a²). The magnetic field outside the wire (r > a) is given by B_out = (μ₀ I) / (2π r). We need the ratio of magnetic fields B and B' at radial distances a/2 and 2a. At r = a/2 (inside the wire): B = (μ₀ I (a/2)) / (2π a²) = (μ₀ I a) / (4π a²) = (μ₀ I) / (4π a). At r = 2a (outside the wire): B' = (μ₀ I) / (2π (2a)) = (μ₀ I) / (4π a). The ratio B/B' = ((μ₀ I) / (4π a)) / ((μ₀ I) / (4π a)) = 1.
Q27 | Mechanics Easy
ok conf:1.00
A particle moves so that its position vector is given by r = cos ω t î + sinωt ĵ. Where ω is a constant. Which of the following is true?
(1) Velocity and acceleration both are perpendicular to r.
(2) Velocity and acceleration both are parallel to r.
(3) Velocity is perpendicular to r and acceleration is directed towards the origin. ✓ Correct
(4) Velocity is perpendicular to r and acceleration is directed away from the origin.
Solution: Given position vector r = (cos ωt) î + (sin ωt) ĵ. Velocity vector v = dr/dt = (-ω sin ωt) î + (ω cos ωt) ĵ. Acceleration vector a = dv/dt = (-ω² cos ωt) î - (ω² sin ωt) ĵ = -ω² ( (cos ωt) î + (sin ωt) ĵ ) = -ω² r. This shows that acceleration is always directed opposite to the position vector, i.e., towards the origin. To check perpendicularity of velocity and position vectors, calculate their dot product: r · v = ((cos ωt) î + (sin ωt) ĵ) · ((-ω sin ωt) î + (ω cos ωt) ĵ) = -ω sin ωt cos ωt + ω sin ωt cos ωt = 0. Since the dot product is zero, velocity is perpendicular to the position vector....
Q28 | Mechanics Easy
ok conf:1.00
What is the minimum velocity with which a body of mass m must enter a vertical loop of radius R so that it can complete the loop?
(1) √gR
(2) √2gR
(3) √3gR
(4) √5gR ✓ Correct
Solution: For a body to successfully complete a vertical loop of radius R, the minimum velocity at the lowest point of the loop (point of entry) is v = √(5gR). At this velocity, the tension in the string or the normal force at the topmost point will be just zero, ensuring the body stays on the circular path.
Q29 | Modern physics Medium
ok conf:1.00
When a metallic surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength λ, the stopping potential is V. If the same surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength 2λ, the stopping potential is V/4. The threshold wavelength for the metallic surface is:
(1) 4λ
(2) 5λ
(3) 5λ/2
(4) 3λ ✓ Correct
Solution: According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, eV_s = hν - W₀ = hc/λ - W₀, where V_s is the stopping potential, W₀ is the work function, and λ is the wavelength. Let W₀ = hc/λ_th, where λ_th is the threshold wavelength. So, eV_s = hc/λ - hc/λ_th. Case 1: eV = hc/λ - hc/λ_th (Eq. 1). Case 2: e(V/4) = hc/(2λ) - hc/λ_th (Eq. 2). Subtract Eq. 2 from Eq. 1: eV - eV/4 = (hc/λ - hc/λ_th) - (hc/(2λ) - hc/λ_th). (3/4)eV = hc/λ - hc/(2λ) = hc/(2λ). So, eV = (4/3)(hc/(2λ)) = (2/3)(hc/λ). Substitute this back into Eq. 1: (2/3)(hc/λ) = hc/λ - hc/λ_th. hc/λ_th = hc/λ - (2/3)(hc/λ) = (1/3)(hc/λ). Therefore,...
Q30 | Thermal physics Mediumdiagram
ok conf:1.00
A gas is compressed isothermally to half its initial volume. The same gas is compressed separately through an adiabatic process until its volume is again reduced to half. Then:
(1) Compressing the gas isothermally will require more work to be done.
(2) Compressing the gas through adiabatic process will require more work to be done. ✓ Correct
(3) Compressing the gas isothermally or adiabatically will require the same amount of work.
(4) Which of the case (whether compression through isothermal or through adiabatic process) requires more work will depend upon the atomicity of the gas.
Solution: The work done during compression is the area under the P-V curve. For a given change in volume, the pressure during adiabatic compression is always higher than during isothermal compression (the adiabatic curve is steeper, P_adiabatic > P_isothermal for the same volume). Therefore, the area under the P-V curve for adiabatic compression is greater than that for isothermal compression. This means that compressing the gas through an adiabatic process will require more work to be done.
[solution diagram]

Q31 | Electromagnetism Easydiagram
ok conf:1.00
A potentiometer wire is 100 cm long and a constant potential difference is maintained across it. Two cells are connected in series first to support one another and then in opposite direction. The balance points are obtained at 50 cm and 10 cm from the positive end of the wire in the two cases. The ratio of emf's is:
(1) 5:1
(2) 5:4
(3) 3:4
(4) 3:2 ✓ Correct
Solution: In a potentiometer, the emf of a cell is directly proportional to the balancing length. Let E₁ and E₂ be the emfs of the two cells. When they are connected in series to support each other, the net emf is E₁ + E₂. The balancing length is L₁ = 50 cm. So, E₁ + E₂ = kL₁ = 50k (where k is the potential gradient). When they are connected in series to oppose each other, the net emf is |E₁ - E₂|. The balancing length is L₂ = 10 cm. So, |E₁ - E₂| = kL₂ = 10k. Assuming E₁ > E₂, we have E₁ - E₂ = 10k. Now we have two equations: 1) E₁ + E₂ = 50k, 2) E₁ - E₂ = 10k. Adding (1) and (2): 2E₁ = 60k => E₁ = 30k...
Q32 | Optics Easy
ok conf:1.00
A astronomical telescope has objective and eyepiece of focal lengths 40 cm and 4 cm respectively. To view an object 200 cm away from the objective, the lenses must be separated by a distance:
(1) 37.3 cm
(2) 46.0 cm
(3) 50.0 cm
(4) 54.0 cm ✓ Correct
Solution: Given focal length of objective f_o = 40 cm, focal length of eyepiece f_e = 4 cm. Object distance from objective u_o = -200 cm. For the objective lens, using the lens formula 1/f_o = 1/v_o - 1/u_o: 1/40 = 1/v_o - 1/(-200). 1/v_o = 1/40 - 1/200 = (5 - 1)/200 = 4/200 = 1/50. So, v_o = 50 cm. The image formed by the objective lens acts as the object for the eyepiece. For normal adjustment (relaxed eye), the final image is formed at infinity. This means the image formed by the objective lens must be at the first focal point of the eyepiece. Thus, the distance of the eyepiece from this intermediate...
[solution diagram]

Q33 | Solid and fluid mechanics Harddiagram
ok conf:1.00
Two non-mixing liquids of densities ρ and nρ (n > 1) are put in a container. The height of each liquid is h. A solid cylinder of length L and density d is put in this container. The cylinder floats with its axis vertical and length pL (p < 1) in the denser liquid. The density d is equal to:
(1) {1 + (n + 1)p}ρ
(2) {2 + (n + 1)p}ρ
(3) {2 + (n - 1)p}ρ
(4) {1 + (n - 1)p}ρ ✓ Correct
Solution: Let A be the cross-sectional area of the cylinder. The total length of the cylinder is L, and its density is d. The weight of the cylinder W = dALg. The cylinder floats, so the buoyant force (F_B) equals its weight. The denser liquid has density nρ and height h. The less dense liquid has density ρ and height h. Given that length pL (p < 1) is immersed in the denser liquid (nρ). This implies that the denser liquid is below the less dense liquid. The remaining length of the cylinder (L - pL) is immersed in the less dense liquid (ρ). So, F_B = (volume immersed in nρ) * nρ * g + (volume immersed i...
[solution diagram]

Q34 | Modern physics Easydiagram
ok conf:1.00
To get output 1 for the following circuit, the correct choice for the input is: [DIAGRAM]
[question diagram]

(1) A = 0, B = 1, C = 0
(2) A = 1, B = 0, C = 0
(3) A = 1, B = 1, C = 0
(4) A = 1, B = 0, C = 1 ✓ Correct
Solution: The circuit consists of an AND gate with inputs A and B, followed by a NOT gate (making it a NAND gate). The output of this NAND gate is Y' = (A·B)'. This Y' then goes to another AND gate along with input C. The final output is Y = Y'·C = (A·B)'·C. We want the final output Y = 1. For Y to be 1, both Y' must be 1 and C must be 1. So, C = 1. For Y' = (A·B)' to be 1, A·B must be 0. This means that A=0 or B=0 or both A=0 and B=0. Let's check option (4): A = 1, B = 0, C = 1. A·B = 1·0 = 0. (A·B)' = 0' = 1. Y = (A·B)'·C = 1·1 = 1. This matches the requirement for the output to be 1.
[solution diagram]

Q35 | Thermal physics Medium
ok conf:1.00
A piece of ice falls from a height h so that it melts completely. Only one-quarter of the heat produced is absorbed by the ice and all energy of ice gets converted into heat during its fall. The value of h is: [Latent heat of ice is 3.4 × 10⁵ J/kg and g = 10 N/kg]
(1) 34 km
(2) 544 km
(3) 136 km ✓ Correct
(4) 68 km
Solution: The potential energy lost by the falling ice of mass m from height h is P.E. = mgh. This energy is converted into heat. Only one-quarter of this heat is absorbed by the ice to melt completely. The heat required to melt the ice is Q_melt = mL, where L is the latent heat of fusion. So, (1/4)mgh = mL. Dividing by m on both sides: (1/4)gh = L. Therefore, h = 4L/g. Given L = 3.4 × 10⁵ J/kg and g = 10 N/kg. h = (4 * 3.4 × 10⁵ J/kg) / (10 N/kg) = 13.6 × 10⁴ m = 136 × 10³ m = 136 km.
Q36 | Gravitation Hard
ok conf:1.00
The ratio of escape velocity at earth (v_e) to the escape velocity at a planet (v_p) whose radius and mean density are twice as that of earth is:
(1) 1:2
(2) 1:2√2 ✓ Correct
(3) 1:4
(4) 1:√2
Solution: The escape velocity is given by v = √(2GM/R). The mass M of a spherical body can be expressed in terms of its density ρ and radius R as M = (4/3)πR³ρ. So, v = √(2G * (4/3)πR³ρ / R) = √( (8/3)GπρR² ) = R√( (8/3)Gπρ ). For Earth: v_e = R_e√( (8/3)Gπρ_e ). For the planet: Given R_p = 2R_e and ρ_p = 2ρ_e. So, v_p = R_p√( (8/3)Gπρ_p ) = (2R_e)√( (8/3)Gπ(2ρ_e) ) = (2R_e)√(2)√( (8/3)Gπρ_e ) = 2√2 * R_e√( (8/3)Gπρ_e ) = 2√2 v_e. The ratio v_e/v_p = v_e / (2√2 v_e) = 1/(2√2). So the ratio is 1:2√2.
Q37 | basics and laboratories Easy
ok conf:1.00
If the magnitude of sum of two vectors is equal to the magnitude of difference of the two vectors, the angle between these vectors is:
(1) 0°
(2) 90° ✓ Correct
(3) 45°
(4) 180°
Solution: Given |A + B| = |A - B|. Squaring both sides: |A + B|² = |A - B|². A² + B² + 2AB cosθ = A² + B² - 2AB cosθ. (Where θ is the angle between vectors A and B). 2AB cosθ = -2AB cosθ. 4AB cosθ = 0. Assuming A and B are non-zero vectors, we must have cosθ = 0. Therefore, the angle θ between the two vectors is 90°.
Q38 | Modern physics Medium
ok conf:1.00
Given the value of Rydberg constant is 10⁷ m⁻¹, the wave number of the last line of the Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum will be:
(1) 0.025 × 10⁴ m⁻¹
(2) 0.5 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
(3) 0.25 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ ✓ Correct
(4) 2.5 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
Solution: The wave number (1/λ) for a spectral line in hydrogen spectrum is given by the Rydberg formula: 1/λ = R (1/n₁² - 1/n₂²). For the Balmer series, the electron transitions to the second energy level, so n₁ = 2. For the 'last line' (or series limit) of the Balmer series, the electron transitions from infinity, so n₂ = ∞. Therefore, the wave number = R (1/2² - 1/∞²) = R (1/4 - 0) = R/4. Given Rydberg constant R = 10⁷ m⁻¹. Wave number = 10⁷ m⁻¹ / 4 = 0.25 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
Q39 | Mechanics Hard
ok conf:1.00
A body of mass 1 kg begins to move under the action of a time dependent force F =(2tî +3t²ĵ) N, where î and ĵ are unit vectors along x and y axis. What power will be developed by the force at the time t?
(1) (2t² + 3t³) W
(2) (2t² + 4t⁴) W
(3) (2t³ + 3t⁴) W
(4) (2t³ + 3t⁵) W ✓ Correct
Solution: Given force F = (2tî + 3t²ĵ) N. Mass m = 1 kg. From Newton's second law, F = ma, so acceleration a = F/m = (2tî + 3t²ĵ) m/s² (since m=1 kg). The body begins to move, implying initial velocity v₀ = 0. Velocity v = ∫a dt = ∫(2tî + 3t²ĵ) dt = (t²î + t³ĵ) m/s. Power P = F · v. P = (2tî + 3t²ĵ) · (t²î + t³ĵ) = (2t * t²) + (3t² * t³) = 2t³ + 3t⁵ W.
Q40 | Electromagnetism Medium
ok conf:1.00
An inductor 20 mH, a capacitor 50 µF and a resistor 40 Ω are connected in series across a source of emf V = 10 sin 340 t. The power loss in A.C. circuit is :
(1) 0.51 W ✓ Correct
(2) 0.67 W
(3) 0.76 W
(4) 0.89 W
Solution: Given L = 20 mH = 20 × 10⁻³ H, C = 50 µF = 50 × 10⁻⁶ F, R = 40 Ω. Source emf V = 10 sin(340t), so peak voltage V₀ = 10 V and angular frequency ω = 340 rad/s. Inductive reactance X_L = ωL = 340 * 20 × 10⁻³ = 6.8 Ω. Capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(ωC) = 1/(340 * 50 × 10⁻⁶) = 1/(0.017) ≈ 58.82 Ω. Impedance Z = √(R² + (X_L - X_C)²) = √(40² + (6.8 - 58.82)²) = √(1600 + (-52.02)²) = √(1600 + 2706.08) = √(4306.08) ≈ 65.62 Ω. The average power loss in an AC circuit is P_avg = (V_rms)² * R / Z² = (V₀/√2)² * R / Z² = (V₀² * R) / (2Z²). P_avg = (10² * 40) / (2 * (65.62)²) = (100 * 40) / (2 * 4306.08) = 400...
Q41 | Mechanics Medium
ok conf:1.00
If the velocity of a particle is u = At + Bt², where A and B are constants, then the distance travelled by it between 1s and 2s is:
(1) A/2 + 4B/3
(2) 3A + 7B
(3) 3A/2 + 7B/3 ✓ Correct
(4) A/2 + B/3
Solution: Given velocity v = At + Bt². The distance travelled x is the integral of velocity with respect to time: x = ∫v dt = ∫(At + Bt²) dt = At²/2 + Bt³/3 + C. Assuming x=0 at t=0, C=0. So, x(t) = At²/2 + Bt³/3. Distance at t=1s: x(1) = A(1)²/2 + B(1)³/3 = A/2 + B/3. Distance at t=2s: x(2) = A(2)²/2 + B(2)³/3 = 4A/2 + 8B/3 = 2A + 8B/3. Distance travelled between 1s and 2s = x(2) - x(1) = (2A + 8B/3) - (A/2 + B/3) = (2A - A/2) + (8B/3 - B/3) = (4A - A)/2 + (8B - B)/3 = 3A/2 + 7B/3.
Q42 | Electromagnetism Easy
ok conf:1.00
A long solenoid has 1000 turns. When a current of 4A flows through it the magnetic flux linked with each turn of the solenoid is 4 x 10⁻³ Wb. The self-inductance of the solenoid is:
(1) 4 H
(2) 3 H
(3) 2 H
(4) 1 H ✓ Correct
Solution: The total magnetic flux linked with a solenoid is given by Φ_total = Nφ, where N is the number of turns and φ is the magnetic flux linked with each turn. The self-inductance L of the solenoid is defined by the relationship Φ_total = LI, where I is the current flowing through it. Given N = 1000 turns, φ = 4 × 10⁻³ Wb, and I = 4 A. First, calculate the total magnetic flux: Φ_total = 1000 * (4 × 10⁻³ Wb) = 4 Wb. Now, use Φ_total = LI: 4 Wb = L * 4 A. So, L = 4 Wb / 4 A = 1 H.
Q43 | Electormagneitsm Easy
ok conf:1.00
A small signal voltage V(t) = V₀ sin ωt is applied across an ideal capacitor C: Which of the following is true?
(1) Current I(t), legs voltage V(t) by 90°
(2) Over a full cycle the capacitor C does not consume any energy from the voltage source ✓ Correct
(3) Current I(t) is in phase with voltage V(t).
(4) Current I(t) leads voltage V(t) by 180°
Solution: In an ideal capacitor, the current I(t) leads the voltage V(t) by a phase angle of 90° (or π/2 radians). The average power consumed by an ideal capacitor over a full cycle is P_avg = V_rms I_rms cos(Φ), where Φ is the phase difference between voltage and current. Since Φ = 90°, cos(90°) = 0. Therefore, P_avg = 0. This means that an ideal capacitor does not consume any energy from the voltage source over a full cycle, it only stores and releases energy.
Q44 | Optics Medium
ok conf:1.00
Match the corresponding entries of column 1 with column 2. [Where m is the magnification produced by the mirror] Column 1: (A) m = -2 (B) m = +1/2 (C) m = +2 (D) m = -1/2 Column 2: (a) Convex mirror (b) Concave mirror (c) Real image (d) Virtual image
(1) A → b and c; B→ b and c; C→ b and d; D→ a and d ✓ Correct
(2) A → a and c; B→ a and d; C→ a and b; D→ c and d
(3) A → a and d; B→ b and c; C→ b and d; D→ b and c
(4) A → c and d; B→ b and d; C→ b and c; D→ a and d
Solution: Magnification m = -v/u. A negative magnification (m < 0) indicates a real and inverted image, which can only be formed by a concave mirror. A positive magnification (m > 0) indicates a virtual and erect image, which can be formed by both concave and convex mirrors. If |m| > 1, the image is magnified; if |m| < 1, the image is diminished; if |m| = 1, the image is of the same size. (A) m = -2: Real, inverted, magnified image. Formed by a concave mirror (b) and is a real image (c). So A → b, c. (B) m = +1/2: Virtual, erect, diminished image. Formed by a convex mirror (a) and is a virtual image (d)...
Q45 | Mechanics Harddiagram
ok conf:1.00
A car is negotiating a curved road of radius R. The road is banked at an angle θ. The coefficient of friction between the tyres of the car and the road is μ_s. The maximum safe velocity on this road is: [DIAGRAM]
(1) √(Rg * (μ_s + tanθ) / (1 - μ_s tanθ))
(2) √(Rg * (μ_s + tanθ) / (1 - μ_s tanθ)) ✓ Correct
(3) Rg * (μ_s + tanθ) / (1 - μ_s tanθ)
(4) Rg * (μ_s + tanθ) / (1 - μ_s tanθ)
Solution: Let N be the normal force and f be the frictional force. The forces acting on the car are its weight mg downwards, normal force N perpendicular to the banked road, and frictional force f along the inclined surface towards the center of the curve (for maximum safe speed). Resolve N and f into horizontal and vertical components. Vertical equilibrium: Ncosθ = mg + fsinθ. Horizontal force provides centripetal force: Nsinθ + fcosθ = mv²/R. For maximum safe velocity, friction acts downwards, opposing the tendency to slide up. If for maximum speed, friction is upwards, then Ncosθ = mg + fsinθ, Nsinθ ...
[solution diagram]

Q136 | Inorganic Chemistry: p-block Medium
ok conf:1.00
Hot concentrated sulphuric acid is a moderately strong oxidizing agent. Which of the following reactions does not show oxidizing behaviour ?
(1) (1) C + 2H2SO4 → CO2 + 2SO2 + 2H2O
(2) (2) CaF2 + H2SO4 → CaSO4 + 2HF ✓ Correct
(3) (3) Cu + 2H2SO4 → CuSO4 + SO2 + 2H2O
(4) (4) 3S + 2H2SO4 → 3SO2 + 2H2O
Solution: CaF2 + H2SO4 → CaSO4 + 2HF In this reaction, oxidation number of none of the atom is not changed. Hence H2SO4 is not acting as oxidising agent.
Q137 | Inorganic Chemistry: Coordination Compound Easydiagram
ok conf:1.00
Which of the following pairs of d-orbitals will have electron density along the axes ?
(1) (1) d_z^2, d_x^2-y^2 ✓ Correct
(2) (2) d_xy, d_x^2-y^2
(3) (3) d_z^2, d_xz
(4) (4) d_xz, d_yz
Solution: d_z^2 and d_x^2-y^2 has electron density concentrated on the axis.
[solution diagram]

Q138 | Inorganic Chemistry: p-block Medium

(2) (2) Square planar, sp³d²
(3) (3) Octahedral, sp³d² ✓ Correct
(4) (4) Trigonal bipyramidal, sp³d
Solution: XeF4, AB4L2→ sp³d2 → geometry → octahedral → shape → square planar
Q139 | Inorganic Chemistry: p-block Medium
ok conf:1.00
Among the following which one is a wrong statement ?
(1) (1) SeF4 and CH4 have same shape ✓ Correct
(2) (2) I3+ has bent geometry
(3) (3) PH5 and BiCl5 do not exist
(4) (4) pπ-dπ bonds are present in SO2
